sandboxFiles.name: [UX Research plan] Project Ollie: Baseline Workflow & Agent Delegation
sandboxFiles.content:
Core Objective
To map the data trails, tool ecosystems, and context-sharing habits of knowledge workers in the real world, as well as their desires and expectations regarding AI delegation.
Participants
* Location & Availability: Must currently live in the U.S. and be able to attend a 90-minute, in-person study session at the NYC Google Office between July 6th and July 24th with their own laptop.1
* Company Profile: Employed at a private educational institute or a commercial business/organization (excluding public education, nonprofits, and government).1
* Software & Tool Usage:
   * Their company must use Google Workspace (Gmail, Drive, Docs, or Calendar) with official work email addresses.1
   * The "actual work" they do must involve co-editing shared documents and spreadsheets (such as Google Docs or Sheets). Users who primarily manage tasks in dedicated project management tools (like Jira, Asana, or Trello) are disqualified.1
* Role & Authority: Must have a role in their business's decision to purchase digital tools and software (either as a sole decision-maker, part of a collective group, or as an influencer).1
* Collaboration Level: They must spend at least half or more of their work week collaborating with others on projects, tasks, or shared goals.1
Details here: Ollie current needs and painpoints 


Research Questions 
* RQ1 (The Fragmented Truth): What does a knowledge worker's true "to-do list" actually look like across formal tools (Jira, Asana) and informal channels (Slack stars, open browser tabs, mental notes)?
* RQ2 (Context Breadcrumbs): When a user moves a task forward, what specific artifacts (links, documents, specific chat sentences) represent the "proof of work" that Ollie will eventually need to ingest?
* RQ3 (The Translation Gap): How do users naturally summarize a complex day of work into a project update? What details do they filter out, and what do they consider critical?
* RQ4 (Agent Delegation Triggers): What tasks do users actually want to hand off versus what they want to keep control over? What parameters (triggers, boundaries, outputs) define a successful Sojo Agent assignment?
Session script
Artifact & Task Audit (15 mins)
* Goal: Uncover the hidden, informal task ecosystems.
* Activity: Have them open their laptop and show you their current "active stack."
* Prompts to use:
   * "Show me the 3 things you know you have to do today but aren't written down in a formal project board."
   * "Look at your open browser tabs or flagged emails right now—what tasks do those represent?"
   * "Where is the conversation happening about your top priority right now? Can you show me that Slack thread/email chain?"
The "Human Ollie" Walkthrough (25 mins)
* Goal: Treat the user's memory and screen as the database to see what Ollie should have done.
* Activity: Walk backward through their last 24–48 hours of work. Have them literally point at things on their screen.
* Prompts to use:
   * "You opened this Google Doc at 10 AM and left a comment. If Ollie was a smart assistant watching this, what is the 'core project update' it should extract from that action?"
   * "If an AI wrote an update based purely on this Slack thread you just showed me, what would it miss? What's the unwritten context here?"
   * "Look at this completed task. What were all the scattered pieces of data (spreadsheets, links, client feedback) that were required to get it done?"
AI Agents tasks (15 mins)
* Goal: Use their real data to define the scope and boundaries of the agents.
* Activity: Review the real tasks identified in Phase 1 and 2. Print out blank "Sojo Agent Job Description" cards (or use a digital whiteboard) and fill them out together.
* Prompts to use:
   * "Look at this specific task you spent an hour on yesterday. If an AI Agent could have done it, what is the exact trigger to start it? (e.g., 'When a new CSV lands in this folder' or 'When a client pings me via email')."
   * "What does the agent need access to on your laptop to successfully do this?"
   * "What is the exact output you expect? A draft email? A spreadsheet update? And where should it put it?"

sandboxFiles.name: A New Drive (Project Folder Agents) PRD.doc
sandboxFiles.content:
# Product Requirement Document (PRD): A New Drive (Project Folder Agents)

## 1. Executive Summary & Vision

Google Drive is the world’s leading cloud storage platform, yet most users do not fully leverage AI features in their daily file workflows. Currently, the "Help Me Organize" feature sees an attach rate of less than 0.1%, and the overall AI attach rate for AI Mode/Projects sits at just 0.7%. 

Our vision is to **10x productivity at work by turning static folders into dynamic, collaborative workspaces powered by AI Agents**. Rather than introducing complex new conceptual models, we will overlay AI capabilities onto a construct users already understand and use daily: the folder. 

This project aims to deliver a reimagined, agent-friendly Drive environment that allows humans and AI agents to seamlessly store, organize, collaborate, and build together.

## 2. Target Audience

### Phase 1 MVP (Alpha/Labs)
*   **Primary Persona:** AI-savvy Small and Medium Businesses (SMBs) and individual creators.
*   **Account Type Focus:** Personal or free Google Workspace accounts. 
    *   *Strategic Note:* Early enterprise/corporate ("Dasher") testing faces significant security restrictions and admin console blockages (such as the third-party Drive API SDK block). Focusing on consumer/personal business accounts allows rapid iteration and unblocked user testing.

### Phase 2 (Long-term)
*   All Google Workspace users, including large enterprise domains with fully integrated compliance and whitelisting controls.

## 3. Goals & Non-Goals

### Goals
*   **Drive Engagement:** Raise the folder-level AI engagement/attach rate from <1% to over 10% for opt-in beta users.
*   **Agent-Friendly Infrastructure:** Build a repository that makes Drive the best place to host, create, and manage third-party AI artifacts (such as web apps, markdown, HTML native pages, and state files).
*   **Rapid Prototyping Path:** Establish a stable, high-velocity testing environment using a React-based frontend hosted in Zipline with automated access expirations.

### Non-Goals
*   To replace the existing Google Drive UI for all enterprise customers on Day 1.
*   To store sensitive user-generated data on unverified external databases (to avoid complex regulatory and legal hurdles).

## 4. Key Features & Requirements

### 4.1 Folder-Level Agents (Team Project Manager)
The core capability of the new Drive is the folder-level agent, which acts as an assistant or independent collaborator on behalf of a group.
*   **Task Suggestion & Tracking:** The agent analyzes the contents of a folder and suggests tasks for team members.
*   **Draft Generation & Revision:** The agent can synthesize drafts (e.g., merging input from three different Google Docs inside the folder) and incorporate teammate feedback automatically.
*   **Activity and Context Digests ("Project Catch-Me-Up"):** An AI Inbox experience scoped directly to the folder, showing what changes humans and agents have made, with the ability to undo or revert agent actions.

### 4.2 Hosting Third-Party AI Artifacts
Drive must evolve beyond storing traditional document types to support interactive AI components.
*   **Embedded Web App & Markdown Rendering:** Folders must natively render markdown and lightweight HTML web apps directly within the Drive UI interface.
*   **Key-Value Store (KVS):** Provide a native state-management layer (e.g., Firebase or Google Sheets backend) so interactive widgets and agents can maintain state directly inside a Drive folder.
*   **Collaboration Features:** Support live cursors, comments on AI artifact previews that persist across updates, and session-level version histories.

### 4.3 High-Value Basic UX Improvements
To make the new Drive experience feel premium and efficient, we will bundle key non-AI usability improvements:
*   **Three-Column View:** A modern sidebar navigation, file list, and active file detail/preview layout.
*   **Embedded File Previewer:** Instant, fast-loading, interactive previewing of diverse file types without opening new tabs.
*   **Pinned Folders:** Better quick-access navigation for active projects.

## 5. Engineering, Security & Privacy Compliance

To successfully execute this program while maintaining Google's strict security standards during public-facing research, the prototype architecture will adhere to the following guardrails:

*   **OAuth & GCP Setup:** Prototype testing will utilize Zipline hosting due to its built-in automated expiration rules and version control capabilities.
*   **Data Minimization Protocol:** To circumvent heavy legal and privacy review pipelines for external UXR, the prototype must avoid storing user-generated data. 
    *   All user session data will either be processed locally on the client-side or securely wiped from the temporary Firebase instance immediately upon user logout.
*   **Whitelist Limitations:** GCP project whitelisting will be managed tightly within the 100-user limit. If larger cohorts are required, the engineering team will automate migration to a secondary GCP project instance.
*   **Future G3 Convergence:** In parallel, UX engineering is exploring a long-term transition to Google 3 to easily leverage native Aurora UI elements and Gemini agentic capabilities.

## 6. Release & Testing Phasing

### Phase 1: Prototype Hackathon (June / July)
*   **Objective:** Align on high-level architecture (Cello + Powertrain stack) and prove that UX engineers can easily tweak pixels and resolve accessibility issues independently of backend engineers.
*   **Deliverable:** A functional, internal URL running in React that hooks into live, personal Workspace APIs so testers can experience the agent acting on real (opted-in) files.

### Phase 2: UXR Diary Study & SMB Private Beta
*   **Objective:** Validate that the folder-level agent actually solves organization and collaboration friction.
*   **Deliverable:** A 100-user whitelist study targeting independent small business owners using personal accounts.

### Phase 3: Gradual Integration
*   **Objective:** Migrate validated "New Drive" concepts (such as the three-column layout or the folder-level summary agent) directly into the core, production Google Drive experience.
